policy_statement: TMG will add and strengthen flood control measures for the Toei Subway based on newly announced expected inundation areas and examine measures for early restoration from large-scale floods.
mechanism_description: The city will add and strengthen flood control and early-restoration measures for the subway to reduce inundation damage and shorten service disruptions during large-scale floods.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (flooding) → protective/rapid-recovery infrastructure measures → ↓ exposure & vulnerability → ↓ flood damage + ↓ service disruption
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system: The city upgrades subway flood defenses (e.g., barriers, waterproofing, drainage/pumping) and implements early-restoration planning to keep tunnels/stations/critical systems functional or quickly recover after inundation.  
(2) Mediating process: Physical floodwater exclusion/management and improved system resilience reduce inundation depth/duration and accelerate repair/restore of power, signaling, and station access.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower flood impacts on transportation—reduced asset damage, reduced downtime, improved continuity of mobility and emergency access during extreme precipitation/flood events.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced exposure and sensitivity of transit infrastructure and riders to flood hazards, resulting in less damage and shorter service disruption during floods.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Flood hazard acting on transit assets is met with protective and rapid-recovery measures that reduce damage and disruption—an exposure/vulnerability reduction pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While improved transit reliability could indirectly affect mode choice and emissions, the direct and intended effect is managing climate hazard impacts, not reducing GHGs or energy use.  
3) Edge cases applied: If flood works were paired with major energy-efficiency upgrades or electrification, those would be separate mitigation mechanisms; here, the mechanism is explicitly flood control/early restoration.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism—flood protection and rapid restoration for transit infrastructure—primarily functions to reduce flood damage/service disruption, which is adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any secondary mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits (e.g., avoiding replacement emissions, avoiding car trips during outages) require policy-specific evidence and are not used for the primary category.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential secondary mitigation co-benefits (avoided reconstruction emissions; avoided temporary shift to cars during transit outages) can occur, but they are not the dominant climate-relevant effect of this mechanism.
key_indicators: “add and strengthen flood control measures”; “Take necessary additional measures for the Toei Subway”; “new expected inundation areas”; “heaviest possible rainfall”; “Strengthen measures against inundation”; “early restoration from large-scale floods”; “overflowing of the Arakawa River”
co_benefits: Improved transit reliability and safety during non-climate disruptions; reduced general maintenance/repair costs from water damage; enhanced emergency response access via continued subway operations.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be partly read as a planning/strategy commitment (“examine measures for early restoration”), but the dominant action is implementing/strengthening physical flood-control measures for subway assets, so infrastructure-investment is the best single instrument type.
